content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 9,
1993
John A.
Slezak
Mitchell,
Silberberg
& Knupp
Trident Center
11377 West Olympic Boulevard
Los Angeles,
CA 90084-1683
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
A—93-364
Dear Mr.
Slezak:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act.
1
FACTS
You are writing on behalf of your law firm, Mitchell,
Silberberg
& Knupp
(“MSK”), which represented the Republican Party
of California
(“RPC”)
in litigation that occurred in 1993.
RPC,
at the conclusion of the case,
owed $40,794.04 to MSK.
You have
indicated that MSK made a business decision to “write down” the
bill to $30,000.
RPC then requested MSK to reduce the bill an
additional $5,000 to $25,000,
in return for a table for ten
persons to a fund—raising event for the RPC called the Reagan
Dinner Tribute on September 14,
1993.
Tickets to this event would
ordinarily have cost $1,000 apiece.
MSK agreed to this additional
$5,000 reduction in return for the table.
OUESTIONS
1.
Has MSK made an in-kind contribution of $5,000 to the
RPC by agreeing to reduce its bill from $30,000 to $25,000 in
return for a table at a fundraising event?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, , g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Page
2
Slezak, John A.
A—9 3 —364
2.
Would the initial reduction from $40,794.04 to
$30,000.00 be considered a contribution?
3.
Which of these actions should be taken into account to
determine whether MSK is a major donor in 1993,
and what should
MSK advise RPC to report on its own statements?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
MSK made an in-kind contribution of $5,000 to the RPC by
agreeing to reduce its bill from $30,000 to’ $25,000 in return for
tickets to an RPC fundraising event.
2.
The initial reduction of the bill from $40,794.04 to
$30,000 would not be considered a contribution if it was made for
bona fide business considerations and not for political purposes.
3.
Provided that MSK’s decision to reduce its bill to
$30,000 was made exclusively for business reasons rather than for
political purposes, it should only count the $5,000 reduction of
its bill,
in return for tickets to the RPC fundraiser, to
determine if it is a major donor in 1993.
It should also inform
the RPC that it received an in-kind contribution of $5,000 in the
form of a reduction of its bill for legal services.
ANALYSIS
The Act defines a contribution,
in part,
as “a payment
except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that it is not made for political purposes.”
(Section 82015.)
“Payment” includes the “rendering of
... property,
services or
anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible.”
(Section
82044.)
The Commission has provided some general guidelines to
determine whether or not the elimination or reduction of a debt
would be considered a contribution.
Candidates and committees
whose debts are reduced would not be receiving contributions so
long as:
1.
the decision to reduce the debt is a bona fide business
judgmEnt that the full amount is uncollectable
2.
the assessment and reduction of fees is applied in a
standard manner to all clients who have not paid their bills,
and
3.
reasonable efforts have been made by the creditor to
collect the full amount of the debt.
(See Hansen Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-103; Lowell Advice
Letter,
No. A-89-702; Steinberq Advice Letter,
No. A-86-
344, copies enclosed.)


Page
3
Slezak,
John A.
A—93--364
However,
when circumstances suggest that the creditors intended to
bestow a political benefit on the debtor or that reasonable steps
were not taken to collect the debts, forgiveness of the debt will
be considered a “contribution.”
If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Wyne Imberi
P6litical Reform Consultant
